paragraphs.text: Having spoken of Noah’s purity, Moses turns to the account of how wicked lust grew strong among Noah’s contemporaries, and says: “And it came to pass, when men began to multiply… and daughters were born unto them” (Gen 6:1). By “many men” Moses here means the tribe of Cain; and by adding “and daughters were born unto them,” he wishes to show that, as we said earlier, the line of Cain was being diminished.

“And the sons of God saw the daughters of men, that they were fair; and they took them wives of all which they chose” (Gen 6:2). By “sons of God” are meant the sons of Seth. They, as children of the righteous Seth, are called the people of God. “The daughters of men” whom the sons of God saw, and whom they found fair, are the daughters of Cain; their beauty and adornment served as a snare for the sons of Seth. The words, “they took them wives of all which they chose,” show that when they took them to wife, they prided themselves before them, made their choice among them: the poor man exalted himself before the rich woman, the old man boasted before the young, even the most unsightly puffed himself up before the fairest. The descendants of Cain paid no heed either to wealth or to outward appearance, but desired only to have tillers for their lands, which had been left unsown. The beginning of this was made by the intemperate and the poor; the intemperate were drawn by the beauty of the daughters of men, the poor longed for their wealth. After such men the whole tribe of Seth followed. And since the sons of Seth took for themselves in marriage the daughters of Cain and despised their former wives, the latter ceased to keep purity and modesty which they had observed until then for their husbands’ sake and with them. And as this intemperance spread among men and women, Scripture says: “All flesh had corrupted its way” (Gen 6:12).

“And the Lord God said: My Spirit shall not always strive with man, for that he also is flesh: yet his days shall be an hundred and twenty years” (Gen 6:3) — that is, in this generation life will not continue to nine hundred years, as it had in the earliest generations. “For he is flesh” — for the days of their life are spent in fleshly deeds. “Yet his days shall be an hundred and twenty years” — if they repent during that time, they will be delivered from the wrath that threatens them; but if they do not repent, by their deeds they will bring upon themselves the wrath. Thus God’s goodness gives a hundred and twenty years for repentance to a generation which by righteousness would not deserve it.

After this, Moses writes about the children born of Cain’s daughters from the sons of Seth, and says: “There were giants in the earth in those days; and also after that, when the sons of God came in unto the daughters of men, and they bore children to them: the same became mighty men, men of renown of old” (Gen 6:4). Those born were giants compared to the small-statured tribe of Cain, not to the strong tribe of Seth. In the descendants of Cain men had become small-statured, because the earth, cursed by God, did not give them its strength, but yielded only weak and powerless produce (food), as still happens today, that the earth, fruits, and herbs sometimes give strength and sometimes do not. Since Cain’s descendants — as cursed, as sons of the cursed, and as living on cursed land — gathered and ate such powerless growths of the earth, they themselves became as feeble as that which fed them.

But the sons of Seth, since they were sons of blessing and lived on land bordering paradise, ate also of the abundant and full-strength produce of the earth and had strong bodies. These strong sons of Seth, coming in to the daughters of groaning Cain, brought forth for his tribe renowned giants. By the words “of old” it is meant that for Cain’s tribe were born giants like those primal renowned men — Seth and Enos.

After the account of the giants born among Cain’s descendants, whose wives were beautiful but in comparison with the sons of Seth too small-statured, Moses says: “And the Lord God saw that the wickedness of man was great in the earth, and that every imagination of the thoughts of his heart was only evil continually” (Gen 6:5). Thus, during the years given for repentance, men added sin to sin. “The wickedness of man was multiplied on the earth” — that is, wickedness spread through both tribes. The imagination of their hearts was set “on evil continually,” because they sinned not only from time to time, but constantly and at every hour, ceasing neither by day nor by night to carry out their wicked imagination.

Because of such universal ungodliness, “God said: I will destroy man… both man and beast, and the creeping thing, and the fowls of the air: for I repent that I have made them” (Gen 6:7). God repents not as one who did not foresee that men would come to such corruption, but He wishes by this to show to later generations the great wickedness of the human race, which had come to such a degree of intemperance that it brings to repentance, as it were, the God who repents of nothing. Moreover, the Holy Spirit justified God’s justice, giving us to understand that men were not destroyed by the Flood without cause. If even this Being, who repents of nothing, condescended so far as to say of Himself “I repent,” He spoke thus so that the bold generation, hearing these words, might shudder, and seeds of repentance might fall into the hearts of the obstinate. If there had been any defect in God’s creatures, God would have made a new world and would not have preserved in the ark a creation of whose making He had repented.

But see, God, by saying “I repent,” by this very thing shows that He did not repent! For if God had repented of sinners, why pity the cattle, creeping things, and fowls of the air which had not sinned? If He did not pity them, why did He say “I repent,” when He did not repent? Therefore His pity for the creation, not only of the guilty but also of the innocent, assures us that God spoke of His repentance out of love for sinners, and does not show that God lacked foreknowledge. The Goodness that had made men was grieved that they should perish for their deeds; and if they did not perish, the later generations would through them become ungodly.

When the shortening of human life and God’s repentance did not bring men to trembling and did not awaken in them sorrow for their sins, then God says to Noah: “The end of all flesh is come before me… Make thee therefore an ark of high-growing wood… The length of the ark shall be three hundred cubits, the breadth fifty cubits, and the height of it thirty cubits… and in a cubit shalt thou finish it above… with rooms… of three stories shalt thou make it… and thou shalt pitch it… within and without with pitch” (Gen 6:13–16). Such a heavy labor God laid upon the righteous man, not wishing to bring a flood upon sinners. Where was Noah to obtain such timber? Where the pitch, iron, and tow? With whose hands could he do this? Whence could he find men to help him in the work? Who would have listened to him, when in the human race “all flesh had corrupted its way” (Gen 6:12)? If Noah himself with his household had set about building the ark, would not everyone who saw it have mocked him? Yet Noah set about building the ark in the first of those years which had been given to his contemporaries for repentance, and finished the building in the hundredth year.
